3A_Bibliographic.citation: Borodovsky, J. T., Squeglia, L. M., Mewton, L., & Marsch, L. A. (2024). Longitudinal use patterns of technology subtypes during the transition into early adolescence: Results from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Journal of Adolescent Health, 75, 809-818.

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: Youth Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) - youth self-report
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Self-reported typical weekday and weekend hours per day for each technology subtype; weighted combination ([weekday x 5] + [weekend x 2])/7; dichotomized at >2 hours/day for multilevel models

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Longitudinal trajectories of technology use by subtype (technology use IS the outcome - descriptive/epidemiological)
3D_Outcome.measure_used: Probability of using each technology >2 hours/day; also examined associations with biological sex, parent education, parent income, parent marital status as covariates
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: At age 9/10: 1.2% used social media >2 hrs/day; by age 13/14: 30.2% used social media >2 hrs/day

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Subject-level random intercepts and slopes; family-level random intercepts; site fixed effects; robust standard errors. Outcome: binary (>2 hrs/day vs <=2 hrs/day). Primary predictor: study wave (continuous). Covariates added in adjusted models: biological sex, parent education, parent income, parent marital status. Complete case analysis.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: Biological sex, parent education, combined parent income, parent marital status
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - parent education and combined parent income included as covariates
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used multilevel logistic regression to examine participant-specific changes and between-subject heterogeneity in the probability of engaging with each technology for more than two hours per day.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Per-wave increase in odds of >2 hrs/day social media use (adjusted model)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the subject-specific odds of youth reporting use of a technology >2 hours per day increased each year by anywhere from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16, 1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14, 3.55)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Per-wave increase in odds of >2 hrs/day TV use (adjusted model)
Per-wave increase in odds of >2 hrs/day video game use (adjusted model)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Study wave OR 2.11 (2.04, 2.18) for video game

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Female vs male odds of >2 hrs/day social media (adjusted)
Female vs male odds of >2 hrs/day video games (adjusted)
Income >=200k vs <25k for >2 hrs/day social media
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: >=200,000... social media OR 0.45 (0.34, 0.60) [from Table 2 - highest income vs reference <$25k]

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Range of Spearman correlations between child- and parent-reported hours of technology use
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Child and parent reports were weakly correlated (rs range: 0.13-0.29).

3G_Interpretation.small_ES_acknowledged: N/A - effects are large (ORs for trajectories of use over time)
3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: by identifying when youths start using specific types of technology and measuring their engagement levels, our study provides clinicians with useful data that they can use to help guide families in making informed decisions about technology to support positive developmental outcomes. Furthermore, this information can pinpoint critical periods for clinical assessments and interventions aimed at promoting balanced technology use and healthy psychosocial development.
3G_Interpretation.notes: Authors also caution against placing undue emphasis on exact values and note weighting was not used which could bias estimates.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: This is primarily a descriptive epidemiological study mapping technology use trajectories. The ORs represent change in use over time and demographic associations, not screen-time-to-outcome effects. Authors are relatively measured in their interpretations and include substantive caveats.

3I_Flags.exclusion_flag: POSSIBLE EXCLUSION - technology use is both the exposure and outcome; no health/behavioral outcome tested
3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: This paper describes longitudinal trajectories of technology use (the exposure) and its demographic correlates. It does not test associations between screen time and any mental health, behavioral, or cognitive outcome. It is epidemiological characterization of the exposure variable itself. May not meet inclusion criteria depending on review scope.
